1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Domain model for the expense-sharing (Splitwise) system.
3	
4	This file holds the "nouns": the money value object, the user, the split
5	strategies' inputs/outputs are kept light here, the expense record, and the
6	one exception family. The orchestrator (expense_service.py) coordinates these.
7	
8	The single most important modelling decision lives here: we do NOT store every
9	individual debt edge. Each User owns ONE net balance ledger — a map of
10	"counterparty id -> signed amount". Positive means they owe ME, negative means
11	I owe THEM. This is the Information Expert principle: the balance between A and
12	B is owned in exactly ONE consistent place, mirrored on both users.
13	"""
14	
15	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
16	from decimal import Decimal
17	from enum import Enum
18	
19	
20	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
21	# One exception family for the whole domain. Callers can catch the base
22	# `ExpenseError` to mean "any expected business failure", or a specific subclass
23	# to react to one failure mode. We NEVER raise bare ValueError/RuntimeError from
24	# our own code — a typed error tells the caller exactly what went wrong.
25	# 🎙️ "Every failure in this system is one of these typed errors. The interviewer
26	#     can catch the base to mean 'business rule broke' or a subclass to react to
27	#     one specific case."
28	class ExpenseError(Exception):
29	    """Base class for every expected failure in the expense domain."""
30	
31	
32	class UnknownUserError(ExpenseError):
33	    """Raised when an operation names a user id that was never registered."""
34	
35	
36	class InvalidSplitError(ExpenseError):
37	    """Raised when a split is malformed: shares don't sum to the total,
38	    percentages don't sum to 100, a payer/participant is unknown, or the
39	    amount is not positive."""
40	
41	
42	class SelfExpenseError(ExpenseError):
43	    """Raised when a single-participant expense names only the payer — there is
44	    nobody to owe anybody, so it is a no-op we reject rather than silently drop."""
45	
46	
47	class SettlementError(ExpenseError):
48	    """Raised when a settlement is invalid: non-positive amount, paying yourself,
49	    or paying more than you actually owe the other person."""
50	
51	
52	class SplitType(Enum):
53	    """The three ways the requirements say an expense can be divided.
54	
55	    🎙️ 'These are the only three split rules in scope: equal, exact amounts,
56	        and by percentage. Each becomes one Strategy class.'"""
57	
58	    EQUAL = "EQUAL"
59	    EXACT = "EXACT"
60	    PERCENT = "PERCENT"
61	
62	
63	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
64	# Money must never be a float. 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3 in float, and a cent of drift in
65	# a balance graph is a real bug. We wrap Decimal so the rest of the code says
66	# Money(...) and the rounding rule (2 dp, banker's-safe quantize) lives in ONE
67	# place. It is frozen because a money value never changes after it is created.
68	# 🎙️ "Money is a Decimal, never a float — a half-cent of float drift in a
69	#     balance graph is a real production bug. The value object centralises rounding."
70	@dataclass(frozen=True)
71	class Money:
72	    """An immutable money amount, stored as a 2-decimal-place Decimal."""
73	
74	    amount: Decimal
75	
76	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
77	        # WHY: normalise on construction so every Money in the system is already
78	        #      rounded to cents. Callers can pass int/str/Decimal freely.
79	        object.__setattr__(self, "amount", self._to_cents(self.amount))
80	
81	    @staticmethod
82	    def _to_cents(value: Decimal) -> Decimal:
83	        # WHY: quantize to 2 dp once, here, so equality and sums are exact.
84	        return Decimal(value).quantize(Decimal("0.01"))
85	
86	    @classmethod
87	    def of(cls, value: float | int | str | Decimal) -> "Money":
88	        # WHY: a friendly constructor so callers never touch Decimal directly.
89	        return cls(Decimal(str(value)))
90	
91	    @classmethod
92	    def zero(cls) -> "Money":
93	        return cls(Decimal("0.00"))
94	
95	    def is_positive(self) -> bool:
96	        return self.amount > Decimal("0.00")
97	
98	    def __add__(self, other: "Money") -> "Money":
99	        return Money(self.amount + other.amount)
100	
101	    def __sub__(self, other: "Money") -> "Money":
102	        return Money(self.amount - other.amount)
103	
104	    def __str__(self) -> str:
105	        return f"${self.amount}"
106	
107	
108	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
109	# A User is the Information Expert for ITS OWN net balances. `_ledger` maps a
110	# counterparty id to a signed Money: positive = that person owes ME, negative =
111	# I owe that person. Every balance change goes through apply_delta, so the rule
112	# "my view of B is always the exact negative of B's view of me" is enforced in
113	# one method instead of scattered across the service.
114	# 🎙️ "Each user owns a ledger of net balances keyed by the other person's id.
115	#     Positive means they owe me. I never store individual receipts — just the net."
116	@dataclass
117	class User:
118	    """A person who can pay for and participate in expenses, and who owns the
119	    net amount they owe / are owed against every other person."""
120	
121	    id: str
122	    name: str
123	    # WHY: a plain dict, not a list of debt rows. O(1) lookup of "what's my net
124	    #      with B", and simplifying debts is one pass over the values. The
125	    #      counterparty's id is the key; the value is signed Money.
126	    _ledger: dict[str, Money] = field(default_factory=dict)
127	
128	    def balance_with(self, other_id: str) -> Money:
129	        # WHY: missing key means we have never transacted -> net zero.
130	        return self._ledger.get(other_id, Money.zero())
131	
132	    def apply_delta(self, other_id: str, delta: Money) -> None:
133	        # WHY: the ONE place a balance changes. Add the signed delta, then drop
134	        #      the entry entirely when it nets to zero so the graph stays sparse
135	        #      (no clutter of $0.00 edges to people you're square with).
136	        new_balance = self.balance_with(other_id) + delta
137	
138	        if new_balance.amount == Decimal("0.00"):
139	            self._ledger.pop(other_id, None)
140	        else:
141	            self._ledger[other_id] = new_balance
142	
143	    def creditors(self) -> dict[str, Money]:
144	        # WHY: people I OWE (my balance with them is negative). Used by reports
145	        #      and by the settle path to validate "you can't pay more than you owe".
146	        return {oid: bal for oid, bal in self._ledger.items() if bal.amount < 0}
147	
148	    def debtors(self) -> dict[str, Money]:
149	        # WHY: people who owe ME (positive balance).
150	        return {oid: bal for oid, bal in self._ledger.items() if bal.amount > 0}
151	
152	    def net_position(self) -> Money:
153	        # WHY: sum of all edges. Across the whole system these must sum to zero —
154	        #      that's the global invariant the concurrency test checks.
155	        total = Money.zero()
156	
157	        for bal in self._ledger.values():
158	            total = total + bal
159	        return total
160	
161	
162	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
163	# A Split is the per-participant share the strategy produced: "user X owes this
164	# slice of the expense". It is frozen because once an expense is recorded the
165	# shares are history — nothing should mutate them.
166	@dataclass(frozen=True)
167	class Split:
168	    """One participant's computed share of an expense."""
169	
170	    user_id: str
171	    share: Money
172	
173	
174	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
175	# An Expense is the immutable record of "who paid, how much, and how it was
176	# divided". We keep it for an audit trail / history listing. The balances it
177	# caused already live on the users; this is the receipt, frozen for all time.
178	# 🎙️ "The Expense is the frozen receipt. The money effect already lives on the
179	#     users' ledgers — this is just the audit record of what happened."
180	@dataclass(frozen=True)
181	class Expense:
182	    """An immutable record of a recorded expense and its computed splits."""
183	
184	    id: str
185	    description: str
186	    payer_id: str
187	    amount: Money
188	    split_type: SplitType
189	    splits: tuple[Split, ...]
190

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Split strategies — the pluggable rule for how an expense is divided.
3	
4	This is the Strategy seam. The service never hardcodes "divide equally"; it asks
5	a SplitStrategy to turn (total, payer, participants, raw weights) into a list of
6	per-participant Splits. Adding a "split by shares/quantity" rule tomorrow is a
7	new class here and zero changes in the service.
8	
9	Each strategy is also the validator for its OWN rule: EXACT checks the parts sum
10	to the total, PERCENT checks they sum to 100. That keeps each rule's correctness
11	next to the rule itself (Information Expert), not in a giant if/else in the service.
12	"""
13	
14	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
15	from decimal import Decimal
16	
17	from models import InvalidSplitError, Money, Split
18	
19	
20	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
21	# The abstract seam. Everything the service knows is `compute(...) -> Splits`.
22	# It does not know or care whether the rule is equal, exact or percentage.
23	# 🎙️ "This is the Strategy interface. The service depends only on this method —
24	#     it has no idea which of the three rules is wired in."
25	class SplitStrategy(ABC):
26	    """Turns an expense total + raw weights into concrete per-user shares."""
27	
28	    @abstractmethod
29	    def compute(
30	        self,
31	        total: Money,
32	        participant_ids: list[str],
33	        weights: list[Decimal] | None,
34	    ) -> list[Split]:
35	        """Return one Split per participant. `weights` carries the raw inputs
36	        (exact amounts for EXACT, percentages for PERCENT, ignored for EQUAL).
37	        Raises InvalidSplitError if the rule's own constraint is violated."""
38	        raise NotImplementedError
39	
40	
41	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
42	# Equal split. The only subtlety is that an N-way split of an odd amount does
43	# not divide evenly (10.00 / 3 = 3.333...). We give everyone the floor share and
44	# hand the leftover pennies to the first participants so the parts sum EXACTLY
45	# to the total — never a lost or invented cent.
46	class EqualSplitStrategy(SplitStrategy):
47	    """Divides the total into equal parts, distributing leftover cents fairly."""
48	
49	    def compute(
50	        self,
51	        total: Money,
52	        participant_ids: list[str],
53	        weights: list[Decimal] | None,
54	    ) -> list[Split]:
55	        n = len(participant_ids)
56	
57	        # WHY: integer cents avoids float drift. 1000 cents / 3 = 333 each,
58	        #      remainder 1 cent goes to the first participant.
59	        total_cents = int((total.amount * 100).to_integral_value())
60	        base = total_cents // n
61	        remainder = total_cents - base * n
62	
63	        splits: list[Split] = []
64	
65	        for index, user_id in enumerate(participant_ids):
66	            cents = base + (1 if index < remainder else 0)
67	            share = Money(Decimal(cents) / Decimal(100))
68	            splits.append(Split(user_id=user_id, share=share))
69	        return splits
70	
71	
72	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
73	# Exact split. The caller already supplied each person's dollar amount; this
74	# strategy's job is to VALIDATE that those amounts add up to the total before we
75	# touch any balance. If they don't, we reject — never record a half-correct expense.
76	class ExactSplitStrategy(SplitStrategy):
77	    """Uses caller-supplied exact amounts; rejects if they don't sum to total."""
78	
79	    def compute(
80	        self,
81	        total: Money,
82	        participant_ids: list[str],
83	        weights: list[Decimal] | None,
84	    ) -> list[Split]:
85	        if weights is None or len(weights) != len(participant_ids):
86	            raise InvalidSplitError(
87	                "EXACT split needs one amount per participant"
88	            )
89	
90	        shares = [Money(w) for w in weights]
91	        summed = Money.zero()
92	
93	        for share in shares:
94	            summed = summed + share
95	
96	        if summed.amount != total.amount:
97	            # WHY: this is the guard that makes the balance graph trustworthy —
98	            #      if the parts don't equal the whole, the books would never net.
99	            raise InvalidSplitError(
100	                f"EXACT shares sum to {summed} but expense total is {total}"
101	            )
102	        return [
103	            Split(user_id=uid, share=share)
104	            for uid, share in zip(participant_ids, shares)
105	        ]
106	
107	
108	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
109	# Percentage split. The caller gives percentages that must sum to 100. We
110	# validate that, then convert each percentage into money using the SAME
111	# leftover-cent distribution as equal split so the parts sum exactly to the total.
112	class PercentSplitStrategy(SplitStrategy):
113	    """Uses caller-supplied percentages (must sum to 100), converts to money."""
114	
115	    def compute(
116	        self,
117	        total: Money,
118	        participant_ids: list[str],
119	        weights: list[Decimal] | None,
120	    ) -> list[Split]:
121	        if weights is None or len(weights) != len(participant_ids):
122	            raise InvalidSplitError(
123	                "PERCENT split needs one percentage per participant"
124	            )
125	
126	        if sum(weights) != Decimal("100"):
127	            raise InvalidSplitError(
128	                f"percentages sum to {sum(weights)} but must sum to 100"
129	            )
130	
131	        total_cents = int((total.amount * 100).to_integral_value())
132	        # WHY: floor each person's cents first, then hand out the rounding
133	        #      remainder one cent at a time so the sum is exactly the total.
134	        raw = [total_cents * w / Decimal("100") for w in weights]
135	        floored = [int(x) for x in raw]
136	        remainder = total_cents - sum(floored)
137	
138	        splits: list[Split] = []
139	
140	        for index, user_id in enumerate(participant_ids):
141	            cents = floored[index] + (1 if index < remainder else 0)
142	            share = Money(Decimal(cents) / Decimal(100))
143	            splits.append(Split(user_id=user_id, share=share))
144	        return splits
145

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""The orchestrator for the expense-sharing system.
3	
4	ExpenseService is the brain: it owns the users, the expense history, and the
5	split-strategy registry. It is the only thing that mutates two users' ledgers
6	together, so it is where atomicity must live.
7	
8	CONCURRENCY ANGLE FOR THIS PROBLEM — deadlock-free lock ordering.
9	A settlement between A and B touches TWO users' balances. If thread-1 settles
10	A->B and locks A then B, while thread-2 settles B->A and locks B then A, they
11	deadlock: each holds one lock and waits for the other forever. The fix is to
12	ALWAYS acquire the two per-user locks in a consistent global order (sorted by
13	user id), so no two threads can ever request them in opposite orders. That makes
14	a lock-ordering cycle impossible by construction.
15	
16	The clean core below is single-threaded and lock-free: `_lock_pair` is a no-op
17	context manager. ThreadSafeExpenseService overrides ONLY that hook with real
18	per-user RLocks acquired in sorted order. The core never changes.
19	"""
20	
21	import itertools
22	import threading
23	from collections.abc import Iterator
24	from contextlib import contextmanager
25	from decimal import Decimal
26	
27	from models import (
28	    Expense,
29	    InvalidSplitError,
30	    Money,
31	    SelfExpenseError,
32	    SettlementError,
33	    Split,
34	    SplitType,
35	    UnknownUserError,
36	    User,
37	)
38	from strategies import (
39	    EqualSplitStrategy,
40	    ExactSplitStrategy,
41	    PercentSplitStrategy,
42	    SplitStrategy,
43	)
44	
45	
46	class ExpenseService:
47	    """Clean, single-threaded core. Owns users, expense history, strategies."""
48	
49	    def __init__(self) -> None:
50	        # WHY: the registry of everything the orchestrator can enumerate and look
51	        #      up. Users by id, the full expense history by id, and the strategy
52	        #      table that maps a SplitType to the object that knows the rule.
53	        self._users: dict[str, User] = {}
54	        self._expenses: dict[str, Expense] = {}
55	        self._strategies: dict[SplitType, SplitStrategy] = {
56	            SplitType.EQUAL: EqualSplitStrategy(),
57	            SplitType.EXACT: ExactSplitStrategy(),
58	            SplitType.PERCENT: PercentSplitStrategy(),
59	        }
60	        # WHY: a counter, not random UUIDs, so test assertions read cleanly
61	        #      (exp-1, exp-2). In production this would be a UUID.
62	        self._expense_seq = itertools.count(1)
63	
64	    # ── concurrency hook ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
65	    # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean version. The thread-safe subclass
66	    #      overrides it to lock the two users in a consistent global order.
67	    #      Zero lock code lives in this file — that is the whole point.
68	    # 🎙️ "My core has no locks at all. The thread-safe version is a subclass that
69	    #     overrides this one hook to take both user locks in sorted order."
70	    @contextmanager
71	    def _lock_pair(self, first_id: str, second_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
72	        yield
73	
74	    # ── registration ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
75	    def add_user(self, user_id: str, name: str) -> User:
76	        """Register a user. Re-registering the same id returns the existing one
77	        so the call is safe to repeat."""
78	        existing = self._users.get(user_id)
79	
80	        if existing is not None:
81	            return existing
82	        user = User(id=user_id, name=name)
83	        self._users[user_id] = user
84	        return user
85	
86	    def get_user(self, user_id: str) -> User:
87	        """Look up a user or raise UnknownUserError."""
88	        user = self._users.get(user_id)
89	
90	        if user is None:
91	            raise UnknownUserError(f"no such user: {user_id}")
92	        return user
93	
94	    # ── the pipeline: record an expense ────────────────────────────────────────
95	    def add_expense(
96	        self,
97	        description: str,
98	        payer_id: str,
99	        amount: Money,
100	        split_type: SplitType,
101	        participant_ids: list[str],
102	        weights: list[Decimal] | None = None,
103	    ) -> Expense:
104	        """Record an expense: validate, compute shares via the chosen strategy,
105	        push the resulting balance deltas onto every participant's ledger, and
106	        store the immutable Expense record. Returns the Expense."""
107	        self._validate_expense(payer_id, amount, participant_ids)
108	        strategy = self._strategies[split_type]
109	        splits = strategy.compute(amount, participant_ids, weights)
110	        self._verify_shares_sum(splits, amount)
111	        self._apply_expense_to_ledgers(payer_id, splits)
112	
113	        expense = Expense(
114	            id=f"exp-{next(self._expense_seq)}",
115	            description=description,
116	            payer_id=payer_id,
117	            amount=amount,
118	            split_type=split_type,
119	            splits=tuple(splits),
120	        )
121	        self._expenses[expense.id] = expense
122	        return expense
123	
124	    def _validate_expense(
125	        self, payer_id: str, amount: Money, participant_ids: list[str]
126	    ) -> None:
127	        # WHY: validate at the boundary BEFORE mutating any ledger. Reject unknown
128	        #      ids, non-positive amounts, duplicate participants, and the degenerate
129	        #      "only the payer participates" case.
130	        if not amount.is_positive():
131	            raise InvalidSplitError(f"expense amount must be positive, got {amount}")
132	
133	        self.get_user(payer_id)
134	
135	        if len(set(participant_ids)) != len(participant_ids):
136	            raise InvalidSplitError("participant list has duplicates")
137	
138	        for participant_id in participant_ids:
139	            self.get_user(participant_id)
140	
141	        others = [pid for pid in participant_ids if pid != payer_id]
142	
143	        if not others:
144	            raise SelfExpenseError(
145	                "an expense needs at least one participant other than the payer"
146	            )
147	
148	    @staticmethod
149	    def _verify_shares_sum(splits: list[Split], amount: Money) -> None:
150	        # WHY: defence in depth. The strategy is supposed to return shares that
151	        #      sum to the total; we re-check here so a buggy future strategy can
152	        #      never silently corrupt the books.
153	        summed = Money.zero()
154	
155	        for split in splits:
156	            summed = summed + split.share
157	
158	        if summed.amount != amount.amount:
159	            raise InvalidSplitError(
160	                f"computed shares sum to {summed}, expected {amount}"
161	            )
162	
163	    def _apply_expense_to_ledgers(
164	        self, payer_id: str, splits: list[Split]
165	    ) -> None:
166	        # WHY: for each participant who is NOT the payer, that participant now
167	        #      owes the payer their share. We push the mirrored signed delta onto
168	        #      BOTH users so the two views stay exact negatives of each other.
169	        payer = self.get_user(payer_id)
170	
171	        for split in splits:
172	
173	            if split.user_id == payer_id:
174	                continue
175	
176	            if not split.share.is_positive():
177	                continue
178	            participant = self.get_user(split.user_id)
179	            # payer's view of participant goes UP (participant owes payer more)
180	            payer.apply_delta(split.user_id, split.share)
181	            # participant's view of payer goes DOWN (owes payer more)
182	            participant.apply_delta(payer_id, Money.zero() - split.share)
183	
184	    # ── the settle path (the concurrency-critical operation) ────────────────────
185	    def settle(self, from_id: str, to_id: str, amount: Money) -> None:
186	        """`from_id` pays `to_id` `amount`, reducing what from owes to. Validates
187	        the amount is positive, the users differ and exist, and from does not pay
188	        more than they actually owe to. Mutates BOTH ledgers atomically."""
189	        self._validate_settlement(from_id, to_id, amount)
190	
191	        # WHY: hand BOTH affected users to the lock hook in their natural order.
192	        #      The hook is the SINGLE place that imposes the consistent global
193	        #      ordering — in the clean core it is a no-op; the thread-safe subclass
194	        #      sorts the two ids and acquires in that order, which is what makes a
195	        #      reciprocal A<->B (or cyclic A->B->C->A) deadlock impossible.
196	        with self._lock_pair(from_id, to_id):
197	            payer = self.get_user(from_id)
198	            payee = self.get_user(to_id)
199	            owed = payer.balance_with(to_id)
200	            # owed is negative when `from` owes `to`. The most `from` can pay is
201	            # the magnitude of that debt.
202	            max_payable = Money.zero() - owed
203	
204	            if amount.amount > max_payable.amount:
205	                raise SettlementError(
206	                    f"{from_id} owes {to_id} only {max_payable}, "
207	                    f"cannot settle {amount}"
208	                )
209	            # paying DOWN the debt: from's balance with to moves UP toward zero,
210	            # to's balance with from moves DOWN toward zero. Mirror exactly.
211	            payer.apply_delta(to_id, amount)
212	            payee.apply_delta(from_id, Money.zero() - amount)
213	
214	    def _validate_settlement(
215	        self, from_id: str, to_id: str, amount: Money
216	    ) -> None:
217	        # WHY: same boundary discipline as the expense path. Reject before locking.
218	        if not amount.is_positive():
219	            raise SettlementError(f"settle amount must be positive, got {amount}")
220	
221	        if from_id == to_id:
222	            raise SettlementError("cannot settle with yourself")
223	        self.get_user(from_id)
224	        self.get_user(to_id)
225	
226	    # ── read / report paths ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
227	    def balance_between(self, from_id: str, to_id: str) -> Money:
228	        """Signed net balance from `from`'s perspective: positive means `to` owes
229	        `from`; negative means `from` owes `to`."""
230	        self.get_user(to_id)
231	        return self.get_user(from_id).balance_with(to_id)
232	
233	    def balance_sheet(self, user_id: str) -> dict[str, Money]:
234	        """Every non-zero balance for a user, keyed by counterparty id."""
235	        user = self.get_user(user_id)
236	        return dict(user._ledger)
237	
238	    def simplify_debts(self, user_ids: list[str]) -> list[tuple[str, str, Money]]:
239	        """Collapse the net balances among a set of users into the minimum-ish set
240	        of transfers (greedy: match biggest debtor to biggest creditor). Returns a
241	        list of (debtor_id, creditor_id, amount) suggested payments. Pure read —
242	        it does NOT mutate ledgers."""
243	        nets: dict[str, Decimal] = {}
244	
245	        for user_id in user_ids:
246	            nets[user_id] = self.get_user(user_id).net_position().amount
247	
248	        transfers: list[tuple[str, str, Money]] = []
249	        # WHY: greedy biggest-creditor / biggest-debtor matching. With net
250	        #      balances this is one pass, O(n log n) for the sort — far cheaper
251	        #      than reconciling every individual debt edge.
252	        while True:
253	            creditor = max(nets, key=lambda k: nets[k])
254	            debtor = min(nets, key=lambda k: nets[k])
255	
256	            if nets[creditor] <= Decimal("0") or nets[debtor] >= Decimal("0"):
257	                break
258	            transfer = min(nets[creditor], -nets[debtor])
259	            nets[creditor] -= transfer
260	            nets[debtor] += transfer
261	            transfers.append((debtor, creditor, Money(transfer)))
262	        return transfers
263	
264	    def expense_history(self) -> list[Expense]:
265	        """All recorded expenses, in insertion order."""
266	        return list(self._expenses.values())
267	
268	
269	class ThreadSafeExpenseService(ExpenseService):
270	    """Thread-safe variant. The ONLY change from the core is the `_lock_pair`
271	    hook: it takes the two users' RLocks in a consistent global order (sorted by
272	    id) so two reciprocal settlements (A->B and B->A) can never deadlock."""
273	
274	    def __init__(self) -> None:
275	        super().__init__()
276	        # WHY: one lock PER user, created lazily. A separate `_map_lock` guards
277	        #      the dict of locks itself so two threads creating a new user's lock
278	        #      at the same instant cannot corrupt the dict.
279	        self._user_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
280	        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()
281	
282	    def _lock_for(self, user_id: str) -> threading.RLock:
283	        # WHY: lock the MAP before reading/creating the per-user lock — otherwise
284	        #      two threads adding the same new key could race on the dict.
285	        with self._map_lock:
286	            lock = self._user_locks.get(user_id)
287	
288	            if lock is None:
289	                lock = threading.RLock()
290	                self._user_locks[user_id] = lock
291	            return lock
292	
293	    def _after_first_lock(self) -> None:
294	        # WHY: a no-op seam between acquiring the first and second lock. In
295	        #      production it does nothing. A concurrency test overrides it to
296	        #      sleep here — that widens the dangerous window (hold lock 1, wait
297	        #      for lock 2) so the deadlock-free ordering is genuinely exercised,
298	        #      not accidentally serialised away by fast lock acquisition.
299	        return None
300	
301	    @contextmanager
302	    def _lock_pair(self, first_id: str, second_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
303	        # WHY: THIS is the single place the global lock ordering is decided. We
304	        #      sort the two ids and acquire strictly low-then-high. Because every
305	        #      thread in the whole system requests its two locks in the SAME order,
306	        #      no hold-and-wait cycle can form — that is what makes deadlock
307	        #      impossible, even for reciprocal (A<->B) or cyclic (A->B->C->A) settles.
308	        low_id, high_id = sorted((first_id, second_id))
309	        low_lock = self._lock_for(low_id)
310	        high_lock = self._lock_for(high_id)
311	
312	        low_lock.acquire()
313	        try:
314	            self._after_first_lock()  # widen the window (no-op in production)
315	            high_lock.acquire()
316	            try:
317	                yield
318	            finally:
319	                high_lock.release()
320	        finally:
321	            low_lock.release()
322

232	def test_cyclic_settlements_no_deadlock_and_net_zero() -> None:
233	    """The classic deadlock setup: a 3-user debt CYCLE (a owes b, b owes c, c owes
234	    a). Threads settle along each edge at the same instant through a Barrier. The
235	    edges are (a,b), (b,c), (c,a) — note (c,a) sorts to (a,c), so the three
236	    settlements request the per-user locks in DIFFERENT orders. Without the global
237	    sorted lock ordering, T1 holding a+b while T2 holds b+c while T3 holds c+a is a
238	    textbook lock-ordering cycle -> deadlock. Sorting every acquisition by user id
239	    breaks the cycle. We assert no thread hangs and every settle succeeds.
240	
241	    We override `_after_first_lock` to sleep BETWEEN the two lock acquisitions.
242	    That forces the dangerous interleaving: every thread holds its first lock and
243	    is reaching for its second at the same instant. With an unsorted ordering this
244	    reliably deadlocks; with the sorted ordering it cannot."""
245	
246	    class SlowOrderingService(ThreadSafeExpenseService):
247	        def _after_first_lock(self) -> None:
248	            time.sleep(0.02)  # hold lock 1, then reach for lock 2 — danger window
249	
250	    svc = SlowOrderingService()
251	
252	    for uid in ("a", "b", "c"):
253	        svc.add_user(uid, uid.upper())
254	
255	    # Build the debt cycle. Each expense makes the participant owe the payer.
256	    # a pays -> b owes a; reverse the roles to make a owe b instead: we want
257	    # a owes b, b owes c, c owes a. Use a payer who is NOT the debtor:
258	    #   b pays expense with a -> a owes b   (edge a->b)
259	    #   c pays expense with b -> b owes c   (edge b->c)
260	    #   a pays expense with c -> c owes a   (edge c->a)
261	    svc.add_expense("e1", "b", Money.of("200.00"), SplitType.EXACT, ["a"], [Decimal("200.00")])
262	    svc.add_expense("e2", "c", Money.of("200.00"), SplitType.EXACT, ["b"], [Decimal("200.00")])
263	    svc.add_expense("e3", "a", Money.of("200.00"), SplitType.EXACT, ["c"], [Decimal("200.00")])
264	    # confirm the cycle: a owes b 200, b owes c 200, c owes a 200
265	    assert svc.balance_between("a", "b") == Money.of("-200.00")
266	    assert svc.balance_between("b", "c") == Money.of("-200.00")
267	    assert svc.balance_between("c", "a") == Money.of("-200.00")
268	
269	    # Each edge gets several threads settling $1 along it, all released together.
270	    edges = [("a", "b"), ("b", "c"), ("c", "a")]
271	    per_edge = 20
272	    n = len(edges) * per_edge
273	    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
274	    errors: list[Exception] = []
275	    wins: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
276	    lock = threading.Lock()
277	
278	    def worker(from_id: str, to_id: str) -> None:
279	        barrier.wait()  # all 60 hit settle() at the same instant
280	
281	        try:
282	            # the in-service _after_first_lock hold makes every thread sit in the
283	            # danger window simultaneously — the sorted ordering is what stops the
284	            # cycle, not luck or accidental serialisation.
285	            svc.settle(from_id, to_id, Money.of("1.00"))
286	
287	            with lock:
288	                wins.append((from_id, to_id))
289	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — record ANY failure for assertion
290	            with lock:
291	                errors.append(exc)
292	
293	    threads = []
294	
295	    for from_id, to_id in edges:
296	
297	        for _ in range(per_edge):
298	            # WHY: daemon=True so that IF a wrong lock ordering deadlocks the
299	            #      workers, this test still FAILS cleanly on the assert below
300	            #      instead of hanging the whole process — daemon threads do not
301	            #      block interpreter exit.
302	            thread = threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(from_id, to_id), daemon=True)
303	            threads.append(thread)
304	
305	    for thread in threads:
306	        thread.start()
307	    # WHY: join against a SINGLE wall-clock deadline (not 10s per thread). A wrong
308	    #      lock ordering would deadlock the workers; this caps the whole wait at
309	    #      ~10s and turns the latent deadlock into a visible, asserted failure
310	    #      instead of a 60x10s hang.
311	    deadline = time.monotonic() + 10.0
312	
313	    for thread in threads:
314	        remaining = deadline - time.monotonic()
315	        thread.join(timeout=max(0.0, remaining))
316	
317	    alive = [t for t in threads if t.is_alive()]
318	    assert not alive, f"DEADLOCK: {len(alive)} threads never finished"
319	    assert errors == [], f"unexpected settlement errors: {errors}"
320	    assert len(wins) == n
321	
322	    # each edge had 20 x $1 settled against a 200.00 debt -> 180.00 remains, and
323	    # it remains a clean cycle, so every user's net position is still zero.
324	    assert svc.balance_between("a", "b") == Money.of("-180.00")
325	    assert svc.balance_between("b", "c") == Money.of("-180.00")
326	    assert svc.balance_between("c", "a") == Money.of("-180.00")
327	    total = sum(
328	        (svc.get_user(u).net_position().amount for u in ("a", "b", "c")),
329	        Decimal("0"),
330	    )
331	    assert total == Decimal("0.00")

336	    Exactly the full $30 must be deducted — no settlement lost, none double-applied
337	    — proving the two-user mutation is atomic under the per-user locks."""
338	    svc = ThreadSafeExpenseService()
339	    svc.add_user("a", "A")
340	    svc.add_user("b", "B")
341	    # a pays a 60.00 expense equally -> b owes a 30.00.
